questions.prompt: Complete: "My father ___ the car every Saturday."
questions.explanation: My father = he, and wash ends in -sh, so add -es: My father washes the car. "washing" is the -ing form (not the simple present), "washs" spells it wrong (you need -es after -sh, not just -s), and "wash" forgets the third-person ending.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct word order: "___ before bed."
questions.explanation: Adverbs of frequency (always, never, usually, often) go before the main verb in a present-simple statement: She always reads a book. Placing always after the verb ("reads always") is incorrect, and fronting it without inverting the sentence ("Always she…") sounds unnatural at A1.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative: "Our teacher ___ French in class."
questions.explanation: Our teacher = she/he, so the negative auxiliary is doesn't + base verb: doesn't speak. "don't" is for I/you/we/they, "doesn't speaks" double-marks the third-person -s (it already sits on does), and "not speak" has no auxiliary at all.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct question: "What time ___ they finish school?"
questions.explanation: They is a plural subject, so the auxiliary is do: What time do they finish school? Does is only for he/she/it, and is/are belong to the verb be — they cannot form a present-simple question with the verb finish.

options.text: He fly to Paris twice a year.
He flys to Paris twice a year.
He flies to Paris twice a year.
He is fly to Paris twice a year.

questions.explanation: He is third person, and fly ends in consonant + y, so y → i + es: He flies to Paris. "fly" forgets the -s ending, "flys" uses the wrong spelling (y must change before -es), and "is fly" wrongly mixes two verbs.

The error is (d): my sister = she, so the auxiliary must be doesn't, not don't — the correct sentence is My sister doesn't eat meat. Sentences (a), (b) and (c) all match the auxiliary to the subject correctly.
